--- SOURCE 1: Supply Chain Diversification 101: Boosting Business Resilience ---
URL: https://gembah.com/blog/supply-chain-diversification/

SUMMARY:
Supply chain diversification is crucial for business resilience, helping companies manage risks and maintain operational agility amid global trade uncertainties. Zara’s use of EU-based production facilities allowed for shorter lead times and swift adaptation to market shifts, while Patagonia’s emphasis on diversified, environmentally friendly suppliers underscores the growing importance of sustainability in supply chain decisions. To achieve effective supply chain diversification, you can implement strategies such as identifying multiple suppliers, leveraging emerging markets, and embracing digital technologies for flexibility and responsiveness. Building resilient supply chains requires navigating the complexities of diversification, from identifying reliable suppliers to exploring emerging markets and leveraging digital technologies. Gembah understands the importance of building resilient supply chains and can help you develop a customized diversification strategy aligned with your goals and risk appetite.

Supply Chain Diversification 101: Boosting Business Resilience
==============================================================

Supply chain diversification spreads risk by using multiple suppliers and locations, crucial in today’s unpredictable market. This article explains why it’s important and offers strategies to implement it effectively.

Overview
--------

*   Supply chain diversification is crucial for business resilience, helping companies manage risks and maintain operational agility amid global trade uncertainties.
*   Effective diversification strategies include multisourcing, leveraging emerging markets, and embracing digital technologies to enhance supply chain performance and responsiveness.
*   Building strong relationships with multiple suppliers, understanding regulatory challenges, and performing thorough cost-benefit analyses are key to successfully implementing supply chain diversification.

Strengthening Your Supply Chain
-------------------------------

The winds of global trade have never been more unpredictable, with trade barriers and supply chain disruptions reshaping the business landscape. The global pandemic illustrated this volatility, putting a spotlight on the risks of relying on a single country or a limited supplier base for critical supplies. In this crucible of change, companies are recognizing the importance of [supply chain management](https://gembah.com/blog/supply-chain-strategies/) and, more critically, the need for chain diversification.

Through the expansion of supply chain networks and enhancement of manufacturing and distribution capabilities, businesses can create a strategic approach. This approach balances sourcing options, reduces risk, and boosts operational agility, acting as a safeguard against the domino effect of concentrated shocks.

The Importance of Supply Chain Diversification
----------------------------------------------

A diverse supply chain resembles a well-balanced financial portfolio, spreading risk and providing fallback options when an asset underperforms. Greater diversity in supply chain resiliency allows businesses not only to survive unexpected events but also to thrive in them. This diversification is more than a good-to-have; it’s a strategic necessity in today’s interconnected global economy.

The aim is to transform potential vulnerabilities into competitive advantages and ensure diversified supply chains are robust, responsive, and competitive.

With **nearly a quarter of the GDP decline** during the pandemic attributed to supply chain issues, the message is clear: greater diversification is not just beneficial, it’s essential for the survival and success of businesses across multiple industries.

Strategies for Effective Supply Chain Diversification
-----------------------------------------------------

The saying, ‘don’t put all your eggs in one basket,’ holds for businesses which need to spread their supply chain assets to evade catastrophic losses. A flexible supply chain that can navigate dynamic market conditions is the fundamental pillar of resilient supply chains. Creating such a chain requires a mix of strategies, each designed to fortify the weak links and seize emerging opportunities.

Strategies for diversification span from geographical spread to technological innovation. Let’s examine these strategies and understand how they contribute to the structure of a resilient and responsive supply chain.

### Identifying Multiple Suppliers

Standing at the heart of supply chain diversification is the concept of multisourcing. It’s the practice of engaging with a broad supplier ecosystem, including a primary supplier, spanning multiple countries and regions, to mitigate the risks of supply chain disruptions from natural disasters or geopolitical upheavals.

This approach has been embraced by industries globally, with many manufacturers seeking to boost their supplier base, with **77% of businesses surveyed** by [Ernst and Young](https://www.ey.com/en_it/automotive-transportation/why-global-industrial-supply-chains-are-decoupling) looking to increase their number of suppliers within two years.

Not just a knee-jerk reaction to recent events, this is a calculated move to balance flexibility with manageability, as seen in Apple’s strategic move to secure contracts with several suppliers for its memory chips, rather than relying on a single source.

### Leveraging Emerging Markets

The lure of emerging markets goes beyond their potential customer base; they are also burgeoning hubs of innovation and manufacturing. By tapping into these markets, manufacturing companies can diversify their supply chains and gain a foothold in new, strategic locations.

[China’s manufacturing](https://gembah.com/countries/china/) sector, for instance, despite producing a staggering **40% of intermediate manufactured goods**, has been investing in clusters and digital technology to remain competitive, while also focusing on the efficient use of raw materials.

Similarly, technology firms are setting up shop in regional manufacturing hubs to stay close to their markets, effectively spreading their risk and increasing their agility.

### Embracing Digital Technologies

In our digital era, the use of advanced technologies is transforming supply chains into intelligent, self-optimizing entities. By harnessing the power of AI and analytics, companies can monitor their supply chain performance in real-time, identifying and responding to disruptions swiftly.

This technological embrace is evident among SMBs, with a significant number using or piloting AI to enhance various aspects of their operations, including supply chains. As digital technologies permeate deeper into the fabric of global supply chains, businesses achieve not just greater resilience but also a competitive edge in the global economy.

Assessing Risk Appetite in Supply Chain Management
--------------------------------------------------

Knowing one’s risk appetite is similar to a captain understanding their ship’s limits in stormy seas. It involves defining the level of turbulence your supply chain can weather before critical functions are affected.

**The [supply chain evaluation](https://gembah.com/services/supply-chain-evaluation/) process includes:**

*   Identifying potential threats, ranging from sole-sourcing risks to tariffs and lengthy lead times.
*   Establishing the required safeguards.

**By conducting a thorough risk assessment, organizations can:**

*   Rank potential risks based on their probability and impact.
*   Develop a risk management plan that incorporates alternative suppliers and backup inventory for crisis scenarios.

Such proactive measures are less costly and disruptive than reactionary fixes, underscoring the importance of establishing resilience from the outset.

![Image 26: Fashion industry management warehouse with clothes.](blob:http://localhost/5f79df6c3b4946584f85779779a1483c)

Building Relationships with Critical Partners
---------------------------------------------

The success of a diversified supply chain depends not only on the number of suppliers but also on the strength of relationships with these partners. Assessing the capability of critical suppliers is crucial, as partners lacking the resources to support a diversification strategy can disappoint even larger organizations.

Through effective supplier relationship management (SRM), businesses can foster positive, productive partnerships that lead to improved product quality, [cost savings](https://gembah.com/blog/the-definitive-guide-to-decreasing-your-wholesale-supplier-costs/), and risk mitigation.

Maintaining open communication, using performance metrics, and leveraging technology to streamline interactions are key to managing these relationships effectively. As Asia Pacific business leaders diversify their supplier base, relationships with partners become ever more crucial in navigating the complexities of global trade.

Overcoming Trade Barriers and Regulatory Challenges
---------------------------------------------------

The journey to supply chain diversification frequently encounters trade barriers and regulatory obstacles, particularly amid geopolitical conflicts and trade wars. Overcoming these challenges is essential for success, as non-compliance can result in fines, limited market access, and damage to reputation.

The implementation of regulations like the Uyghur Forced Labour Prevention Act ([UFLPA](https://www.dhs.gov/news/2024/05/23/enforcing-uyghur-forced-labor-prevention-act)) in the US and the German Supply Chain Due Diligence Act ([SCDDA](https://www.assent.com/resources/knowledge-article/what-is-the-scdda/)), alongside a surge in trade restrictions worldwide, exemplifies the increasing complexity of international trade.

To overcome these barriers, companies must stay abreast of new regulations, adapt their strategies accordingly, and leverage free trade agreements where possible.

Cost-Benefit Analysis of Supply Chain Diversification
-----------------------------------------------------

The decision to undertake supply chain diversification should be weighed carefully; it necessitates a comprehensive cost-benefit analysis (CBA) to confirm the financial feasibility of this strategy. This analysis measures tangible costs, such as labor and manufacturing, against intangible benefits such as customer satisfaction and competitive advantages.

By employing methodologies like the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) or Net Present Value (NPV), businesses can compare different projects and make informed decisions on investments in supply chain diversification.

Case Studies: Successful Supply Chain Diversification
-----------------------------------------------------

Case studies from various industries, including the logistics industry, shed light on the practical application and benefits of supply chain diversification. These real-life examples, ranging from manufacturing to fashion, demonstrate how companies have tackled challenges and capitalized on opportunities to enhance their business resilience through diversification.

Let’s explore some of these success stories, noting the strategies and outcomes that have set these companies apart in their respective fields.

### Manufacturing Industry

In the manufacturing sector, companies like [Kshore](https://hbr.org/2023/11/case-study-how-should-we-diversify-our-supply-chain) and Unilever have turned to supply chain diversification to increase efficiency and navigate risks. As supply chain leaders, Unilever’s consolidation of its warehouses and implementation of CPFR agreements showcases a strategic move towards optimizing asset utilization and inventory management.

Conversely, Kshore’s consideration of shifting [production to Mexico](https://gembah.com/countries/mexico/) highlights the pressures faced by manufacturers to reduce lead times and environmental impacts of shipping.

Both cases demonstrate the critical role of diversification in not only mitigating risks but also in achieving ambitious growth targets.

### Technology Sector

In the fast-paced world of technology, IBM’s predictive analytics models stand out as a testament to the power of digital technologies in fortifying supply chains. By analyzing a myriad of factors, from historical data to market trends, [IBM](https://www.ibm.com/case-studies/ibm-supply-chain) has been able to anticipate disruptions, enabling informed supplier selection and contingency planning.

Similarly, Hewlett Packard Enterprise’s engagement with suppliers to set science-based targets for emissions reduction illustrates how diversification strategies can also align with sustainability goals.

### Auto Industry

The auto industry, with its complex and intertwined supply chains, offers insights into diversification’s role in maintaining production flexibility. Toyota’s [strategic adjustments](https://hbr.org/2022/11/what-really-makes-toyotas-production-system-resilient) to its supply chain, such as diversifying supplier locations and adapting its just-in-time strategy, allowed the company to swiftly switch production and recover faster than competitors during disruptions.

Tesla’s in-house software capabilities further exemplify the benefits of supply chain control and flexibility, as the company was able to diversify its supplier base and adjust to semiconductor shortages with relative ease.

### Fashion Industry

In the fashion industry, where trends and consumer demands change rapidly, Zara’s nearshoring strategy and Patagonia’s sustainable sourcing exemplify successful supply chain diversification. Zara’s use of [EU-based production](https://www.strategyzer.com/library/zara-business-model) facilities allowed for shorter lead times and swift adaptation to market shifts, while Patagonia’s emphasis on diversified, environmentally friendly suppliers underscores the growing importance of sustainability in supply chain decisions.

![Image 28: Fashion industry management warehouse with clothes.](blob:http://localhost/5f79df6c3b4946584f85779779a1483c)

Government Policies and Incentives
----------------------------------

The influence of government in shaping supply chain dynamics is significant. Governments can greatly impact the resilience of supply chains through targeted policies and incentives. Initiatives such as the Department of Energy’s [Office of Manufacturing](https://www.energy.gov/mesc/office-manufacturing-and-energy-supply-chains) and Energy Supply Chains and the Minerals Security Partnership underline the public sector’s dedication to promoting diversification and stability in critical industries.

Such support, coupled with incentives for relocating manufacturing closer to end customers, exemplifies the symbiotic relationship between government action and supply chain resilience.

Building a Resilient Future
---------------------------

In the complex tapestry of global trade, supply chain diversification emerges as a pivotal thread, weaving together strategies, relationships, and innovations into a resilient fabric that can withstand the shocks of an unpredictable market.

From identifying multiple suppliers to leveraging digital technologies and overcoming regulatory challenges, businesses have a suite of tools at their disposal to build a robust supply chain. The case studies examined demonstrate that diversification is not just theory but a practical, achievable goal that leading companies have already embraced.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

#### **Why is supply chain diversification important for companies?**

Supply chain diversification is important for companies because it helps mitigate risks, ensures operational stability during disruptions, and maintains competitive advantage in the global economy. It is crucial for a company’s success and resilience.

#### **What are some strategies for effective supply chain diversification?**

To achieve effective supply chain diversification, you can implement strategies such as identifying multiple suppliers, leveraging emerging markets, and embracing digital technologies for flexibility and responsiveness. These steps help in achieving a diversified and resilient supply chain.

#### **How can assessing risk appetite help in supply chain management?**

# Why Supplier Diversification Is Crucial & How to Do It

## SUMMARY

Discover how leading brands use supplier diversification to reduce risk, manage tariff shocks, and build more resilient, cost-effective supply chains.

When 2025âs Liberation Day tariffs sent global supply chains into a frenzy, it became clear: relying on a single supplier, or even a single country, is no longer a strategyâitâs a risk. Amid growing geopolitical flare-ups, pandemic-era aftershocks, global policies, and natural disasters, supplier diversification has emerged as the go-to strategy for building a resilient supply chain.Â

Consumers donât wait, and margins donât forgive missteps.Â Â

So, how can you expand your supplier base while minimizing disruptions and unlocking cost savings? Letâs unpack the strategies to diversify suppliers that leading brands use to stay nimble, competitive, and disruption-ready.

## The Real-World Cost of Single-Source Supplier Dependency

**According to McKinsey, supply chain disruptions lasting longer than a month or more now occur every** [**3.7 years**](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights/risk-resilience-and-rebalancing-in-global-value-chains) **on average, costing businesses up to 45% of their yearly profits.**

Over the past years, brands across industries have faced a perfect storm: unstable freight markets, port congestion, raw material shortages, and now, renewed tariff threats.

On April 2, 2025, the Trump administration unveiled sweeping trade measures known as the âLiberation Day Tariffsâ: a baseline 10% tariff on nearly all imports, excluding only Canada and Mexico, plus additional reciprocal tariffs targeting about 60 countries deemed to have unfair trade practices.

The announcement immediately triggered volatility across global markets. Businesses scrambled to interpret the implications, accelerate shipments, and rework sourcing strategies. The backlash was swift. Although some of these tariffs were later walked back, the sudden policy reversal created weeks of panic, stockpiling, and pricing volatilityâespecially for U.S. importers that lacked contingency plans.Â

If your sourcing is concentrated in one geography or reliant on a handful of current suppliers, your exposure is enormous. Risk mitigation through supplier diversification ensures that you can adapt swiftly to policy changes and maintain operational continuity.

If your supply chain is over-concentrated in a single region, especially one prone to volatility, you're inviting unnecessary risk.

--- SOURCE 3: How to Safely Diversify Your Supply Chain in 2025 ---
URL: https://www.supplychainbrain.com/articles/41065-how-to-safely-diversify-your-supply-chain-in-2025

SUMMARY:
And while supplier diversification is something that should be done as a way to risk-proof supply chain operations, it has now become something organizations will be forced to do. If companies consistently monitor and manage their current suppliers on a quarterly or yearly basis, they'll create a more effectively de-risked supply chain. Even in environments that would otherwise be risky, you can de-risk your supply chain by making sure you’re incorporating risk assessments, risk management, risk treatments and continuous improvement all the way through the supplier lifecycle. The most resilient supply chains will balance geographic proximity and cost advantages against potential risks — whether they're related to infrastructure reliability, political stability, or operational challenges.

FULL CONTENT:
How to Safely Diversify Your Supply Chain in 2025

Analyst Insight: Geopolitical tensions and unpredictable trade market conditions are forcing companies to rethink their supply chain strategies. And while supplier diversification is something that should be done as a way to risk-proof supply chain operations, it has now become something organizations will be forced to do. because of the pending tariffs proposed by the new U.S. presidential administration.

First steps to supplier diversification include comprehensive mapping. Begin by conducting a detailed analysis of the current supply chain infrastructure, meticulously documenting existing supplier relationships and geographical dependencies. This initial diagnostic step will offer understanding of an organization’s present state, and identify where potential vulnerabilities are now.

Subsequent to mapping is to develop a forward-looking model in terms of risk within the future supply chain, and use that to make decisions based on where to source in the future. Companies should get a head start on it before they’re forced to do it.

Once there’s clarity, and an organization has a good appreciation of the risks, collaboration begins across multiple business functions across that supply chain. Everyone from procurement teams to legal departments (because they deal with supplier contracts), to logistics specialists and supply chain managers (who manage the ongoing supplier relationships) should be aligned.

The goal is to manage the supply not just for efficiency and operations performance, but also to risk-proof the supply chain as a whole.

The diversification process isn’t about replacing existing suppliers, but rather about creating a more resilient and flexible network. If companies consistently monitor and manage their current suppliers on a quarterly or yearly basis, they'll create a more effectively de-risked supply chain. It's less about assuming that suppliers from a particular country or of a specific type are inherently low risk. Instead, the key is recognizing that any supplier can become high-risk if you're not actively tracking their potential vulnerabilities, understanding the specific risks they’re exposed to, and assessing how well they manage those risks.

In other words, any supplier can be at a higher risk if you’re not keeping an eye on them.

Even in environments that would otherwise be risky, you can de-risk your supply chain by making sure you’re incorporating risk assessments, risk management, risk treatments and continuous improvement all the way through the supplier lifecycle.

Outlook: Looking ahead to 2025 and beyond, sophisticated diversification will need to be attuned to risk in order to be successful. While countries like Mexico, Vietnam and India present attractive opportunities for reducing dependency on China, companies must develop comprehensive risk management strategies for new suppliers from the outset. This means thoroughly vetting potential suppliers' risk mitigation plans, establishing redundant supply networks, and understanding the unique infrastructure and business challenges each region presents.

The most resilient supply chains will balance geographic proximity and cost advantages against potential risks — whether they're related to infrastructure reliability, political stability, or operational challenges. Organizations that take this holistic approach to diversification, rather than focusing solely on cost and efficiency metrics, will find themselves better positioned for what 2025 has in store.

# Diversifying Supply Chains: The Role of Development Assistance and Other Official Finance

![Photo: Getty Gallery/Adobe Stock](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/styles/500_x_300/s3/publication/AdobeStock_181027980.jpeg?VersionId=e5Mm_ENKNtkSOzFTzzEpRmtMRul9IFDQ&h=8b0c4e07&itok=T34hqadI)

Photo: Getty Gallery/Adobe Stock

## Table of Contents

## Table of Contents

[Brief](/taxonomy/term/3014)
by 

Conor M. Savoy 
and 
Sundar R. Ramanujam

Published June 10, 2022

### Available Downloads

### **The Issue:**

### **Background**

Diversified supply chains that cut across multiple geographies and generate significant economic benefits for the countries they connect are central to economic globalization. These complex networks of supply chains have contributed to increased stability and prosperity worldwide. They have opened new markets, transforming relationships between the developed and developing world and making supply chain resilience critical to sustained global economic growth. They have also become increasingly concentrated in a handful of geographic regions, such as China. In recent years, however, global supply chains have experienced a series of disruptions that call into question their resilience and have contributed to broader economic uncertainty.

Over the past 30 years, China has emerged as the preeminent global manufacturing hub, producing [14.7 percent](https://unctad.org/news/china-rise-trade-titan) of global exports of goods in 2020 and [28.7 percent](https://www.statista.com/chart/20858/top-10-countries-by-share-of-global-manufacturing-output/) of global manufacturing output in 2019. This is a remarkable three-fold jump from 2004 (just three years after its admission into the World Trade Organization, or WTO), when China’s manufacturing output stood at [8 percent of the global total](https://data.worldbank.org/indicator/NV.IND.MANF.CD?locations=CN-1W). In addition to being the top exporter and manufacturer of goods, the world’s second-largest economy is also the leading trade partner for [128 countries](https://www.lowyinstitute.org/the-interpreter/chart-week-global-trade-through-us-china-lens), making any disruption to its manufacturing sector tremendously consequential to global supply chains. Across all sectors, China remains the largest source of imports for [over a third of the world](https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/china/china-and-the-world-inside-the-dynamics-of-a-changing-relationship) (65 countries) and is the world’s largest trading partner by volume of goods.

Covid-19 put the overreliance on China in stark relief, as countries found themselves unable to access critical supplies needed to respond to the pandemic. For instance, the United States imported [over 97 percent](https://www.cato.org/blog/determining-americas-dependence-china-essential-medical-goods) of essential personal protective equipment (PPE)—including face shields, gloves, and other protective garments—from China. Other countries face similar risks, with [43 percent](https://www.piie.com/blogs/trade-and-investment-policy-watch/covid-19-chinas-exports-medical-supplies-provide-ray-hope) of global imports of PPE exported by China. More than [80 percent](https://www.nytimes.com/2019/05/11/opinion/sunday/generic-drugs-safety.html) of the United States’ supply of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) comes from just China and India. Up to 95 percent of ibuprofen (a common anti-inflammatory drug), 91 percent of hydrocortisone (a common dermatology medication), 70 percent of acetaminophen (used for pains and aches), and over 40 percent of heparin (used to treat blood clots) and penicillin (a critical but commonly used antibacterial drug) come from China. Valued at $74.6 billion in 2017, China’s supply of APIs constitutes over [40 percent of the global API market](https://daxueconsulting.com/api-industry-in-china/), giving Beijing enormous leverage in determining global access to critical and life-saving drugs.

The global economy’s overreliance on China was tested under the Trump administration, for whom adjusting the trade imbalance with China was a critical policy objective. The administration applied tariffs and other trade barriers on China, the United States’ top trading partner. In addition, after the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) reported in June 2018 that U.S. intellectual property worth approximately [$50 billion](https://ustr.gov/about-us/policy-offices/press-office/fact-sheets/2018/june/section-301-investigation-fact-sheet) was being stolen by Chinese state action (or inaction) annually, the United States sought to counteract this economic damage by imposing 25 percent import tariffs on [up to $370 billion in Chinese goods](https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/ustr-announces-opportunity-for-domestic-4473905/). While the Trump administration resorted to crude negotiating tactics (often disrupted by ill-timed tweets by the president), the overall objective of creating greater equity in the U.S.-China trade and economic relationship was—and continues to be—widely shared in Washington. Officials in the Biden administration sought to keep many of these tariffs in place when they entered office in January 2021.

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Taiwan.jpg?.vQg37Se9a.__S9EdvtBnNKP7GHl0B9e)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Taiwan.jpg?.vQg37Se9a.__S9EdvtBnNKP7GHl0B9e)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Taiwan.jpg?.vQg37Se9a.__S9EdvtBnNKP7GHl0B9e)

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Vietnam.jpg?AFVvZmIV1atikbQBXS5kI0FH00BrYSZ.)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Vietnam.jpg?AFVvZmIV1atikbQBXS5kI0FH00BrYSZ.)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Vietnam.jpg?AFVvZmIV1atikbQBXS5kI0FH00BrYSZ.)

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_EU.jpg?RvlVNjeqovMw6hSWvJcanjhNG6lUaErS)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_EU.jpg?RvlVNjeqovMw6hSWvJcanjhNG6lUaErS)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_EU.jpg?RvlVNjeqovMw6hSWvJcanjhNG6lUaErS)

[![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Mexico.jpg?bPdT8RaZrPRESOm81ofY5toZQcFhApWR)](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Mexico.jpg?bPdT8RaZrPRESOm81ofY5toZQcFhApWR)

![](https://csis-website-prod.s3.amazonaws.com/s3fs-public/220610_Savoy_Imports_Mexico.jpg?bPdT8RaZrPRESOm81ofY5toZQcFhApWR)

This economic overreliance, combined with the U.S.-China trade dispute, also disrupted global supply chains as it created [new “winners” and  “losers”](https://carnegieendowment.org/2020/06/24/in-u.s.-china-trade-war-new-supply-chains-rattle-markets-pub-82145) in international commerce. The tariffs boosted U.S. imports of goods produced in Europe, Mexico, Taiwan, and Vietnam. This volatile global trading environment was further exacerbated by the Covid-19 pandemic, which emerged in late 2019 in China. The spread of the virus caused the World Health Organization (WHO) to declare a global public health emergency in January 2020. In response to the uncontrolled spread of Covid-19, governments around the world imposed stringent lockdowns that caused massive spikes in unemployment and crippling economic disruptions. Covid-19 restrictions made production levels drop, created massive bottlenecks at seaports, and precipitated other transportation delays. This caused the door-to-door movement of goods to slow dramatically—by 2022, the average freight container [spent 20 percent more time](https://unctad.org/news/blog-heres-how-we-can-resolve-global-supply-chain-crisis) in transit, leading to supply shortages around the world.

Since early 2021, as pandemic restrictions have eased, employment has recovered significantly and economic prospects have improved. Increased economic growth has created new problems as consumer demand has quickly outpaced supply, which in turn has triggered inflation at levels not seen in many advanced economies in decades. As of February 2022, around [44 percent](https://blogs.worldbank.org/voices/return-global-inflation) of advanced economies and [71 percent](https://blogs.worldbank.org/voices/return-global-inflation) of emerging markets and developing economies were experiencing consumer inflation of 5 percent or more. Compounding all of this are the crippling economic sanctions the United States imposed on Russia for its unprovoked invasion of Ukraine. While the sanctions are a necessary strategic response, they do carry costs for a global economy already reeling from continued supply chain disruptions and inflation by limiting access to critical energy supplies and minerals from Russia.

### **Diversifying Supply Chains**

The supply chain crisis has tempted politicians to look to “onshore” industries by providing financial and other incentives to bring manufacturing back to a country that has lost industrial capacity. However, research by [CSIS and other organizations suggests](https://www.csis.org/analysis/recovery-resilience-diversifying-supply-chains-reduce-risk-global-economy) that such moves will lead to increased costs for consumers and an overall loss for the economy. It likely makes more sense to diversify supply chains through “nearshoring” or by exploring other countries that offer an economically viable alternative. By spreading sourcing, production, and shipping of goods and services across economically and geographically diverse nations, the risk of a single country shocking global supply chains due to natural or political factors is significantly lowered. To that end, bilateral and multilateral development institutions can use a range of tools and financial instruments to incentivize firms to join global efforts to diversify their production and distribution systems. The pandemic-induced supply chain crisis [pushed major economies to publicly](https://www.france24.com/en/europe/20211014-imf-and-g20-urged-to-tackle-supply-bottlenecks-threatening-global-economy) commit to diversifying the supply chains critical to domestic markets and protect the sectors most sensitive to their national interests.

The United States has been taking a few steps in this regard, using the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation (DFC) and the U.S. Export-Import (EXIM) Bank to support diversification efforts. In one prominent initiative in 2021, the DFC announced the award of a [$500 million loan](https://www.dfc.gov/media/press-releases/dfc-announces-approval-provide-500-million-debt-financing-first-solars) to finance the development of a thin-film solar-cell manufacturing facility in India. The deal, pegged as the largest single-debt transaction for the DFC, was one of the United States’ more direct efforts to diversify the supply chains of the critical renewable energy sector. The DFC also partnered with the French Development Agency’s Proparco, the German Investment Corporation, and the International Finance Corporation (IFC), jointly announcing €600 million (approximately $650 million) in financing to boost [South Africa’s Covid-19 vaccine production](https://www.state.gov/dfc-announces-financing-to-support-covid-19-vaccine-manufacturing-in-south-africa/) and help countries in sub-Saharan Africa confront the pandemic. A similar partnership between the DFC, IFC, and the European Investment Bank provided [$14 million](https://pressroom.ifc.org/all/pages/PressDetail.aspx?ID=26493) in grants to [Senegal](https://www.dfc.gov/media/press-releases/dfc-ifc-eib-and-afd-boosting-covid-19-vaccine-production-africa-senegal-based), the first step of the $200 million project to expand vaccine production there.

To combat the threat of Chinese competition in strategic, “transformational” sectors such as biotechnology and semiconductors, the United States has also authorized the EXIM Bank to establish the [China and Transformational Exports Program](https://www.exim.gov/about/special-initiatives/ctep). Under this mandate, the EXIM Bank uses a range of financial instruments to help exporters in “transformational” sectors thrive despite competition from China, where [state-owned financial institutions manipulate factors](https://www.csis.org/analysis/red-ink-estimating-chinese-industrial-policy-spending-comparative-perspective) such as prices, land-acquisition policies, labor conditions, and industrial subsidies to promote Chinese-owned corporations. In November 2021, the EXIM Bank [signed a bilateral agreement](https://www.exim.gov/news/exim-signs-memorandum-understanding-lithuanias-ministry-economy-and-innovation) with Lithuania to deepen economic cooperation. The agreement provided for up to [$600 million](https://eimin.lrv.lt/en/news/mou-signed-with-exim-bank-from-us-enables-diversification-of-supply-chains-and-expansion-of-trade) in loans and guarantees aimed at bolstering Lithuania’s capacity to produce and export lasers, semiconductors, biotechnology, and renewable energy products for U.S. markets, furthering supply chain diversification.

Since the early 2010s, Japan has pursued a policy of diversifying supply chains that support its domestic market activities. However, progress on these efforts has been slow. For example, when China [cut off](https://www.nytimes.com/2010/09/23/business/global/23rare.html) its exports of rare-earth minerals to Japan in 2010 (as part of their dispute over the Senkaku/Diaoyu Islands), the Japanese government [invested in alternative](https://qz.com/1998773/japans-rare-earths-strategy-has-lessons-for-us-europe/) sources of rare-earth minerals, including Kazakhstan, Malaysia, and Namibia, as well as other strategically aligned countries who met a share of Japan’s rare-earth mineral demands and reduced the country’s dependence on China. These efforts only intensified after the Covid-19 pandemic triggered an economic crisis. Starting in 2020, the Japanese government provided incentives—including [$2.2 billion](https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/asia_pacific/japan-helps-87-companies-to-exit-china-after-pandemic-exposed-overreliance/2020/07/21/4889abd2-cb2f-11ea-99b0-8426e26d203b_story.html) in 2020 and an additional [$2.1 billion](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2021-02-03/supply-chains-latest-japan-adds-incentives-to-counter-china-inc) in 2021 in loans and subsidies—for its companies to move their production and distribution capabilities out of China. By April 2021, more than [four out of five](https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Business-trends/Over-80-of-Japan-Inc.-recast-supply-chains-as-pandemic-hit-survey#:~:text=TOKYO%20%2D%2D%20More%20than%2080,by%20highly%20streamlined%20production%20processes.) manufacturing companies in Japan had begun to diversify their supply chains to streamline their production processes and insulate them from exogenous shocks associated with overreliance on a single country. By the end of the year, [more than 81 overseas projects](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2021-02-03/supply-chains-latest-japan-adds-incentives-to-counter-china-inc) operated by Japanese companies receiving this financial incentive decided to open plants and factories in Southeast Asian countries. These included facilities to manufacture Covid-19 test kits in Vietnam and medical rubber gloves in Malaysia.

In support of these efforts, the United States and Japan have committed to [closer cooperation](https://thediplomat.com/2021/07/japan-the-us-and-the-quest-for-secure-supply-chains/) on supply chains in sensitive sectors such as semiconductors and digital infrastructure. In June 2021, the Japanese government operationalized this commitment with its [Strategy for Semiconductors and the Digital Industry,](https://www.meti.go.jp/english/press/2021/0604_005.html) which aims to promote the joint development of advanced foundries in countries around the world. In response to the economic crisis stemming from China’s manufacturing and supply disruptions, the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (Quad)—consisting of Japan, the United States, Australia, and India—also launched the Supply Chain Resilience Initiative in April 2021, which seeks to [de-risk global supply chains](https://www.dfat.gov.au/news/media-release/joint-statement-supply-chain-resilience-initiative-australian-indian-and-japanese-trade-ministers) by enhancing digital technologies in supply chain management and diversifying trade and investment. Through this initiative, the Quad governments have sought to share best practices for building resilience in global supply chains. They have continued to convene on this issue, and in September 2021, the Quad [also agreed](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/statements-releases/2021/09/24/fact-sheet-quad-leaders-summit/) to develop new supply chains to accelerate the development of the renewable energy and semiconductor sectors.

### **Policy Recommendations**

The U.S. government and its allies, including Japan, recognize the need to support greater diversification of supply chains. Covid-19, growing competition with China, and other causes of economic disruptions underscore the need to shift strategic supply chains to friendlier countries. The United States and Japan, alongside their partners in the Quad, should work together to facilitate these shifts. Both countries can provide significant official finance to support these efforts. The two governments should increase cooperation between their development agencies, development finance institutions, and export credit agencies to incentivize private companies to shift their supply chains. Using these economic and political capabilities, Japan and the United States can effectively leverage the long-term strategic investments necessary to transform and de-risk global supply chains for the private sector.

By its nature, the use of public resources to alter prevailing market dynamics carries political risks, as it can create “winners” and “losers.” The use of foreign assistance to diversify global supply chains is no different—even when it serves U.S. strategic and national security interests. The Biden administration can minimize the impact of these risks by ensuring that interventions to alter the global supply chain system are strategic and designed to produce a “win-win” scenario: diversified supply chains for the United States and increased economic growth for developing countries. To that end, this brief makes the following six recommendations for the United States and Japan:

The total value of international trade in 2021 reached nearly [$29 trillion](https://unctad.org/news/global-trade-hits-record-high-285-trillion-2021-likely-be-subdued-2022#:~:text=%E2%80%9COverall%2C%20the%20value%20of%20global,the%20COVID%2D19%20pandemic%20struck.), representing approximately 30 percent of the global economy. More than three-fourths of this value was generated through the trade of goods that rely on global supply chains. Given the size and volume of trade that the current system supports, policymakers will need to remain prudent about diversification efforts. Specifically, the U.S. and Japanese governments should spearhead the development of a framework that can be used to identify economic sectors that should be prioritized for supply chain diversification.

Developing trade capacity among new country partners is mission-critical for any supply chain diversification efforts, as its absence will repel private capital investments. “Trade capacity” refers to the institutional, physical, and human capabilities that allow countries to participate and benefit from international trade. Foreign aid—which can take the form of financial tools and technical assistance—will have a catalytic role in this development. For many, building trade capacity begins with reforming the regulatory system, including export controls and customs processing. Describing itself as the “[largest single-country provider of trade capacity building](https://www.usaid.gov/what-we-do/economic-growth-and-trade/trade-and-regulatory-reform/trade-capacity-buildingaid-trade),” the **U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID)** aims to help developing countries transition their economies, conform with WTO standards, and harmonize their economic and trade practices. In the past two decades, for example, the United States has provided over [$8.9 billion](https://tcb.usaid.gov/trends.html) in assistance to sub-Saharan Africa and more than [$4.7 billion](https://tcb.usaid.gov/trends.html) to Latin America in support of trade capacity building, making the two regions the largest and second-largest recipients of such investments. A 2010 evaluation of USAID’s strategy for trade capacity building estimated that for every dollar invested in a country to build its trade capacity, the country enjoyed [$42 in additional exports](https://www.usaid.gov/sites/default/files/documents/1865/USAID%20TCB%20Policy%209-9-16%20(DRAFT%20for%20public%20comment).pdf) just two years later. In partnership with the [Japan International Cooperation Agency](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/our_work/thematic_issues/private/activity.html), USAID can continue to build on its investments and support countries in their efforts to:

The **U.S. Trade and Development Agency (USTDA)** likewise has a record of helping developing countries make their [transportation sectors and port security systems](https://ustda.gov/success_story/success-in-colombia-supporting-border-safety-and-promoting-economic-growth/) more efficient. The USTDA does this through a twin-pronged strategy: invest in enhanced technological capabilities and share best practices from the United States. In a similar vein, the USTDA can lead in connecting U.S. states with key ports (such as California, Florida, Massachusetts, and New York) with potential trading partners in the developing world, helping equip these countries with advanced data management systems to improve their access to supply chain information.

Technological upgrades can also expedite and secure export capabilities in countries where antiquated customs controls add to the costs for many firms—especially those trading perishable goods, for which the risks of total loss on an investment due to shipping delays are high. By implementing the WTO Trade Facilitation Agreement, these countries can address this deadweight economic loss; one [WTO study](https://www.wto.org/english/res_e/booksp_e/world_trade_report15_e.pdf) estimated that universal implementation of this agreement in good faith could add over $1 trillion to the global economy per year while boosting global exports by up to $3.6 trillion. In particular, **USTR and USAID** can facilitate trade with countries that can be strategically vital trading partners. Both agencies could invest in these countries’ digital technology capabilities to improve their governance, harmonize their import and export standards, reduce the risk of corruption and delays, and consolidate flow-and-control functions for goods into a single-window clearance system.

While donor institutions have the capacity to de-risk new investments made by private firms in developing countries, doing so without addressing governance and corruption issues will lead the investments to fail and even create perverse incentives for firms to adopt fraudulent practices. The Biden administration has recognized that efforts to combat corruption are [critical](https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/presidential-actions/2021/06/03/memorandum-on-establishing-the-fight-against-corruption-as-a-core-united-states-national-security-interest/#:~:text=Corruption%20corrodes%20public%20trust%3B%20hobbles,means%20to%20undermine%20democracies%20worldwide.) to national security interests and that they make global markets fairer and more equitable. Strengthening governance also leads to market stability and regulatory transparency, both of which are necessary conditions for countries to attract capital, especially when setting up new production facilities.

When deciding whether to invest in a country, manufacturing firms consider ease of land acquisition, flexibility within a country’s labor laws, taxation policies, entry and exit requirements, and other national and local laws. These factors carry significant risks to doing business in countries that either lack state capacity or have poor governance practices that contribute to weak rule of law. **USAID** can play a pivotal role in driving the U.S. efforts to fight corruption and build institutional stability in partner countries. To that end, the agency should:

**Build infrastructure capacity.**

The lack of resilient infrastructure in low- and middle-income countries robs the global economy of [$4.2 trillion each year](https://www.worldbank.org/en/news/press-release/2019/06/19/42-trillion-can-be-saved-by-investing-in-more-resilient-infrastructure-new-world-bank-report-finds). Investors expecting a smooth and well-functioning trading system rely on the availability of durable infrastructure, encompassing a network of roads, railways, ports, airports, round-the-clock electricity access, and digital communication systems that can handle heavy data traffic. Therefore, investments in infrastructure are the most direct way governments can intervene to lower financial costs, mitigate business risks, attract private investment, and boost economic growth and net exports. To support developing countries in mobilizing new infrastructure investments, the development community should use a wide range of bilateral and multilateral financial instruments to:

Additionally, the USTDA can play a crucial role in partnering with developing countries and reforming their public procurement processes such that infrastructure projects are assessed holistically through a life-cycle cost analysis. To do this, the USTDA need only scale up its acclaimed **Global Procurement Initiative** from its current roster of 15 partner countries. Moreover, it can partner with the **Japanese Ministry of Economy, Trade, and Industry** to provide officials in developing countries training on best practices for quality infrastructure procurement, [as they did in 2018](https://ustda.gov/ustda-engages-with-japan-on-quality-infrastructure-development/) to train Association of Southeast Asian Nations officials in how to develop energy infrastructure.

Foreign assistance and public sector interventions can be used to attract investment and finance production facilities in new countries outside of China. By spreading out production facilities, firms help diversify supply chains and reduce the global overreliance on one country. **Development finance institutions** (DFIs) are useful in this regard, bringing a range of tools that can be used to:

**Mitigate early-stage risks.**

Firms establishing production facilities in new countries are bound to experience challenges in mobilizing investment capital, as the lack of a robust manufacturing sector in a country raises the firm’s risk profile. DFIs such as the Japan Bank for International Cooperation (JBIC) and DFC—and their counterparts in the United Kingdom, Canada, and European Union—can lower these risks and boost confidence among direct and institutional investors by creating a **blended concessional-finance facility** and helping them overcome the early-stage risks of investment.

DFIs and export credit agencies—such as the Nippon Export and Investment Insurance (NEXI)—can also deploy [credit guarantees](https://www.csis.org/analysis/innovations-guarantees-development), which can help firms improve financing terms (both debt and equity), hedge currency volatility, and increase the viability and profitability of new investments. Similarly, NEXI provides [loan insurance](https://www.meti.go.jp/press/2020/07/20200729005/20200729005-1.pdf) up to 1.5 trillion yen (approximately $11.5 billion) for overseas Japanese subsidiaries to ensure they can access working capital. NEXI also offers [overseas investment insurance](https://www.nexi.go.jp/en/products/types/investment.html) (which indemnifies an overseas Japanese company or joint venture from losses incurred by war or terrorism), which can be used to protect investors supporting new facilities in countries facing [fragility, conflict, and violence](https://www.worldbank.org/en/topic/fragilityconflictviolence/overview). These guarantees and insurance programs are critical to managing and mitigating the early-stage risks for new facilities and help attract firms to countries that are willing to host them but have historically lacked a robust manufacturing economy.

Finally, partial guarantees can help commercial lenders and banks in partner countries with challenging markets support trade and develop an export-import capacity. Guarantees can also help better performing lenders in partner countries securitize their more viable assets, allowing them to recycle capital quickly.

**Diversify MSME supply chains.**

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilize multiple suppliers in various geographic locations to mitigate risks from geopolitical issues, natural disasters, and trade disruptions, ensuring greater operational agility.

enriched_points.point_title: Invest in AI and IoT Technologies
enriched_points.expanded_content: Leverage AI and IoT to enhance supply chain visibility, automate processes, and optimize inventory management, enabling real-time decision-making and responsiveness to market changes.
Foster strong collaborations and transparent communication with suppliers and stakeholders, improving trust and coordination, which is essential for navigating supply chain complexities.

enriched_points.point_title: Focus on Sustainability and Risk Management
enriched_points.expanded_content: Implement sustainable practices within supply chains while establishing robust risk management frameworks to identify, assess, and mitigate potential disruptions effectively.
